title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: The baby ___ all night.
explanation: Cry ends in consonant + y. Change y to i, add -ed → cried.

title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: She ___ her keys on the table.
explanation: Lay (= put down) is irregular. Past simple is laid.

title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: He ___ the ball to his friend.
explanation: Throw is irregular. The past simple is threw (not thrown — that's the participle).

title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: I ___ a letter to my grandmother.
explanation: Write is irregular. The past simple is wrote.

title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: We ___ in the lake last summer.
explanation: Swim is irregular. Past simple is swam (not swum — that's the participle).

title: Past simple: irregular spelling
question: She ___ her coffee too quickly.
explanation: Drink is irregular. Past simple is drank (not drunk — that's the participle).

title: Past simple: did + base form
question: ___ the movie yesterday?
explanation: Past question = Did + subject + base form. Did you see.

title: Past simple: did + base form
question: She didn't ___ the homework.
explanation: After didn't, use the base form. Didn't do.

title: Past simple: did + base form
question: Did he ___ to the party last night?
explanation: After did, use the base form. Did he go.

title: Past simple: did + base form
question: I didn't ___ anything strange.
explanation: After didn't, use the base form. Didn't hear.

title: Past simple: time expression
question: I saw her ___ — last Tuesday, I think.
explanation: Ago follows the time period: two days ago. 'Before two days' and 'ago two days' are not grammatical English word orders.

title: Past simple: time expression
question: We moved here ___.
explanation: Use 'in' with years. We moved here in 2019.

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: I always wake up early ___ the morning.
explanation: Use 'in' with morning, afternoon, evening. In the morning.

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: The stars are beautiful ___ night.
explanation: Exception: we say 'at night,' not 'in the night.'

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: I have a meeting ___ Monday morning.
explanation: Use 'on' with specific days, even with morning/afternoon. On Monday morning.

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: The shop closes ___ midnight.
explanation: Use 'at' with exact times: at midnight, at noon, at 3 o'clock.

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: She was born ___ July.
explanation: Use 'in' with months. In July.

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
question: The concert is ___ the 15th of March.
explanation: Use 'on' with specific dates. On the 15th of March.

title: Prepositions of place: in/on
question: We travelled ___ bus for six hours.
explanation: Travel + means of transport uses 'by + bare noun': by bus, by train, by plane. 'By the bus' and 'by a bus' are not used in this fixed expression.

title: Prepositions of place: in/on
question: The keys are ___ the drawer, not on top of it.
explanation: 'In' shows position inside a container. The other forms are not grammatical English.

title: Prepositions of place: in/on
question: The cat is hiding ___ the bed.
explanation: 'Under' = below. The other forms are not grammatical English.

title: Prepositions of place: in/on
question: The lamp is ___ the table — be careful not to knock it off.
explanation: 'On' = touching the surface from above. The other forms are not grammatical English.

title: Possessives: its vs it's
question: The dog wagged ___ tail.
explanation: Its (no apostrophe) = belonging to it. It's = it is.

title: Possessives: its vs it's
question: ___ raining outside.
explanation: It's = it is. It's raining = It is raining.

title: Possessives: its vs it's
question: The tree has lost ___ leaves.
explanation: Its (no apostrophe) shows possession. It's = it is.

title: Possessives: its vs it's
question: ___ a beautiful day today.
explanation: It's = it is. It's a beautiful day = It is a beautiful day.

title: Possessives: plural
question: The ___ uniforms are in the locker room. (more than one player)
explanation: Plural nouns ending in s take just an apostrophe: players'. The other forms are not grammatical.

title: Possessives: plural
question: The ___ toys are on the floor.
explanation: Children is already plural. Add 's → children's.

title: Possessives: names ending in s
question: That is ___ book.
explanation: For names ending in s, add 's → James's. (James' is also accepted, but James's is standard.)

title: Possessives: names ending in s
question: I went to ___ house for dinner.
explanation: For names ending in s, add 's → Thomas's.

title: Object pronouns: after prepositions
question: She is looking at ___.
explanation: After a preposition (at), use object pronouns. At me, not at I.

title: Object pronouns: after prepositions
question: This present is for ___.
explanation: After a preposition (for), use object pronouns. For him, not for he.

title: Object pronouns: after prepositions
question: Can you sit next to ___?
explanation: After a preposition (next to), use object pronouns. Next to her.

title: Object pronouns: after prepositions
question: There is a message from ___.
explanation: After a preposition (from), use object pronouns. From them.

title: Object pronouns: compounds
question: My mother drove my sister and ___ to school.
explanation: Remove 'my sister and' — you'd say 'drove me,' not 'drove I.' Use me.

title: Object pronouns: compounds
question: My friend and ___ went to the park together.
explanation: Subject position → use I. 'My friend and I went.' The other forms are not grammatical subject pronouns.

title: Object pronouns: compounds
question: The teacher gave ___ and my partner the homework.
explanation: Object position (gave ___) → use me. The teacher gave me the homework.

title: Object pronouns: compounds
question: The waiter brought the bill to my wife and ___.
explanation: After a preposition (to), use the object pronoun. 'To my wife and me.' Test: remove 'my wife and' → 'brought the bill to me.'

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: I'd like some ___, please. (= a piece of meat from a bird)
explanation: Chicken (the meat) is uncountable. Some chicken. Chickens = living birds.

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: There are three ___ on the farm.
explanation: Counting individual birds → use the plural countable form. Three chickens.

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: Can I have a ___ of water?
explanation: Glass (= a drinking container) is countable. A glass of water.

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: The window is made of ___.
explanation: Glass (= the material) is uncountable. Made of glass.

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: The printer is out — I need to buy more ___.
explanation: Paper (= the material) is uncountable. You cannot say 'a paper' or 'a papers' for the material.

title: Countable/uncountable: both
question: Please sign these ___.
explanation: Papers (= documents) is countable. These papers.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ money do you have?
explanation: Money is uncountable. Use how much, not how many.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ chairs are in the room?
explanation: Chairs are countable. Use how many, not how much.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ bread do we need?
explanation: Bread is uncountable. Use how much.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ eggs did you buy?
explanation: Eggs are countable. Use how many.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ furniture is in the living room?
explanation: Furniture is uncountable (even though it can be many items). Use how much.

title: Countable/uncountable: how much/many
question: How ___ pieces of luggage do you have?
explanation: Pieces is countable, so use how many. (Luggage itself is uncountable, but we count pieces.)
